ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies known components (Word2Vec lexicon expansion, IR term-weighting, and standard fusion strategies with SVM/SVR) to the well-studied task of volatility prediction, and compares to relevant baselines (Kogan et al., Wang et al., Tsai et al.). Its use of embedding-augmented IR weighting for long 10-K Risk Factors and sectoral analysis is a useful, non-trivial engineering contribution, but conceptually it mainly combines existing techniques rather than introducing a fundamentally new method or theoretical perspective. From a theory-first viewpoint the absence of formal analysis of when embedding-based IR weighting should help limits the perceived novelty beyond an incremental empirical advance. | B(3): This paper delivers a focused, practical extension—using Word2Vec to expand a finance lexicon and to augment IR term-weighting for long 10-K Risk Factors—that is meaningfully different from classic bag-of-words or static lexicon approaches used in Kogan et al., Wang et al., and Tsai et al. However, the core components (lexicon expansion, embedding-based similarity, and standard fusion strategies like early fusion, stacking, and MKL) are combinations of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new method. The work’s differentiation is clear and practically useful for long financial documents and sectoral analysis, but it represents an incremental, application-driven advance rather than a highly novel theoretical contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies known components (Word2Vec lexicon expansion, IR term-weighting, and standard fusion strategies with SVM/SVR) to the well-studied task of volatility prediction, and compares to relevant baselines (Kogan et al., Wang et al., Tsai et al.). Its use of embedding-augmented IR weighting for long 10-K Risk Factors and sectoral analysis is a useful, non-trivial engineering contribution, but conceptually it mainly combines existing techniques rather than introducing a fundamentally new method or theoretical perspective. From a theory-first viewpoint the absence of formal analysis of when embedding-based IR weighting should help limits the perceived novelty beyond an incremental empirical advance.

However, the core components (lexicon expansion, embedding-based similarity, and standard fusion strategies like early fusion, stacking, and MKL) are combinations of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new method. The work’s differentiation is clear and practically useful for long financial documents and sectoral analysis, but it represents an incremental, application-driven advance rather than a highly novel theoretical contribution.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an NLP/ML problem (text-driven volatility prediction) that fits a top-tier venue: it develops embedding-augmented IR models, applies standard ML fusion methods (early/late/MKL), and evaluates against established baselines (Kogan, Wang, Tsai) on a substantial 10-K and market dataset. However, from a theory-first methodological perspective it relies primarily on empirical engineering (lexicon expansion and IR weighting heuristics) and omits theoretical analysis of when embedding-augmented weighting should help, rigorous statistical significance testing and temporal robustness checks, and full controlled ablations for each claimed contribution (e.g., lexicon expansion vs weighting variants vs fusion). These gaps are not fatal for venue fit but justify caution — the work is a good fit for NLP/ML with minor concerns about the depth of analytical and statistical validation expected at top conferences. | B(4): This is a strong applied NLP paper that fits the agenda of top NLP/ML venues: it uses word-embedding based IR methods, extensive empirical comparisons (Kogan, Wang, Tsai), and multiple fusion strategies (early/late/MKL) to improve volatility forecasting from 10‑K Risk Factors — exactly the type of domain-adapted modeling and rigorous evaluation I value. The work uses appropriate benchmarks (GARCH, market-only baselines), realistic datasets (SEC 10‑Ks 2006–2015 and market data), sectoral analyses, and reports hyperparameters, all supporting reproducibility; my only minor concern is its very domain-specific focus which might make it slightly better suited to an applied NLP/financial ML workshop than a more theory-focused flagship. Overall the contribution is well-positioned for a top-tier applied NLP/ML conference.

However, from a theory-first methodological perspective it relies primarily on empirical engineering (lexicon expansion and IR weighting heuristics) and omits theoretical analysis of when embedding-augmented weighting should help, rigorous statistical significance testing and temporal robustness checks, and full controlled ablations for each claimed contribution (e.g., lexicon expansion vs weighting variants vs fusion). These gaps are not fatal for venue fit but justify caution — the work is a good fit for NLP/ML with minor concerns about the depth of analytical and statistical validation expected at top conferences.

The work uses appropriate benchmarks (GARCH, market-only baselines), realistic datasets (SEC 10‑Ks 2006–2015 and market data), sectoral analyses, and reports hyperparameters, all supporting reproducibility; my only minor concern is its very domain-specific focus which might make it slightly better suited to an applied NLP/financial ML workshop than a more theory-focused flagship. Overall the contribution is well-positioned for a top-tier applied NLP/ML conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (embedding-augmented IR, fusion strategies, sector analysis). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent, and figures/tables/examples support the experimental claims; related work coverage is thorough. Minor presentation issues remain: key GARCH implementation details are relegated to supplementary material and the implications of the stacking/training-split limitation could be explained more clearly. Overall the writing is adequate with only small clarity shortcomings. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents contributions early (embedding-augmented IR sentiment, fusion strategies, sectoral analysis) with a clear methods→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and adequate for reproducibility, though some implementation details (e.g., GARCH) are deferred to supplementary material which slightly interrupts self-containment. Figures/tables and evaluation metrics are reported and seem informative for the claims, and the writing is generally clear with only minor presentation issues (e.g., brief discussion of stacking data-split drawbacks). From an applied NLP/finance perspective the paper communicates its ideas effectively with room for small clarifying edits.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (embedding-augmented IR, fusion strategies, sector analysis). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent, and figures/tables/examples support the experimental claims; related work coverage is thorough. Minor presentation issues remain: key GARCH implementation details are relegated to supplementary material and the implications of the stacking/training-split limitation could be explained more clearly. Overall the writing is adequate with only small clarity shortcomings.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents contributions early (embedding-augmented IR sentiment, fusion strategies, sectoral analysis) with a clear methods→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and adequate for reproducibility, though some implementation details (e.g., GARCH) are deferred to supplementary material which slightly interrupts self-containment. Figures/tables and evaluation metrics are reported and seem informative for the claims, and the writing is generally clear with only minor presentation issues (e.g., brief discussion of stacking data-split drawbacks). From an applied NLP/finance perspective the paper communicates its ideas effectively with room for small clarifying edits.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and important baselines (GARCH, a market-only SVM, and prior text-based methods Kogan et al., Wang et al., Tsai et al.), and uses common metrics (r2, MSE) on the same 10‑K/Yahoo datasets, so overall coverage of competing approaches is good. However, fairness is weakened by omitted GARCH implementation details (moved to supplement) and no mention in the summary of statistical significance testing, temporal robustness checks, or controlled ablations that would attribute gains specifically to the embedding‑augmented IR weighting and fusion strategies. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly and note some limits (e.g., older reports/stacking data issues), but the lack of explicit ablation and significance analyses prevents a fully rigorous comparison. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and influential baselines (Kogan et al. 2009, Wang et al. 2013, Tsai et al. 2014) and against strong market-only and GARCH baselines, using the same 10‑K/Yahoo price data and standard metrics (r2, MSE). Comparisons appear largely fair, though GARCH implementation details were deferred to supplementary material and stacking suffers from a noted reduced training regime, which could affect parity across methods. The authors discuss limitations (concept drift, data-split effects) and sectoral behavior, and they situate their method relative to relevant competing approaches, so I judge comparisons good with minor omissions/detailing issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and important baselines (GARCH, a market-only SVM, and prior text-based methods Kogan et al., Wang et al., Tsai et al.), and uses common metrics (r2, MSE) on the same 10‑K/Yahoo datasets, so overall coverage of competing approaches is good. However, fairness is weakened by omitted GARCH implementation details (moved to supplement) and no mention in the summary of statistical significance testing, temporal robustness checks, or controlled ablations that would attribute gains specifically to the embedding‑augmented IR weighting and fusion strategies. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly and note some limits (e.g., older reports/stacking data issues), but the lack of explicit ablation and significance analyses prevents a fully rigorous comparison.

Comparisons appear largely fair, though GARCH implementation details were deferred to supplementary material and stacking suffers from a noted reduced training regime, which could affect parity across methods. The authors discuss limitations (concept drift, data-split effects) and sectoral behavior, and they situate their method relative to relevant competing approaches, so I judge comparisons good with minor omissions/detailing issues.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the experiments provide promising evidence that embedding-augmented IR sentiment and fusion improve volatility prediction and that baseline comparisons are generally appropriate. However, they also agree there are methodological gaps—missing implementation details, limited ablation/statistical significance reporting, and potential issues with fusion training—that weaken the reliability of the claims. Given these substantiated empirical results but important omissions that limit reproducibility and causal attribution, a cautious mid-range score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents experimental evidence that embedding-augmented IR sentiment and fusion with market features improve volatility prediction, and compares to reasonable baselines (GARCH, prior literature). However, key technical gaps remain: there is no formal analysis or conditions when embedding-based lexicon expansion should help, GARCH implementation details were omitted, and the summary does not report statistical significance testing or the controlled ablations (e.g., isolating lexicon expansion, IR weight variants, fusion strategies) that are essential to attribute gains. Given these methodological omissions and unsupported assumptions about conceptual-drift mitigation, the results are promising but not yet fully substantiated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by extensive experiments comparing embedding-augmented IR sentiment features against strong baselines (Kogan, Wang, Tsai) and market-only models, and by evaluations of multiple fusion strategies and sectoral analyses. Experimental methodology is generally sound (SVM/SVR baselines, r2 and MSE metrics, reported hyperparameters), but there are minor technical weaknesses: key implementation details for GARCH are deferred to supplementary material and stacking may suffer from reduced training data due to the 70/30 split—points that could affect reproducibility and the strength of fusion claims. No mathematical derivations were required beyond standard IR/embedding usage, and conclusions are reasonably justified by the reported results, so only minor issues prevent a top score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers a solid empirical evaluation with multiple baselines, fusion strategies, sector analyses, and reported hyperparameters, and that related work and limitations are well covered. The main shortcomings are reduced reproducibility (missing code, some details in supplementary) and incomplete causal/robustness ablations; these issues prevent a top score but do not undermine the overall methodological contribution, so a score of 4 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments cover multiple baselines (GARCH, prior textual methods), sector analyses, and fusion strategies which gives reasonable breadth to support the main claims, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not indicate controlled ablations isolating each claimed contribution (e.g., explicit tests showing how much gain comes from lexicon expansion vs. IR weighting variants vs. fusion), nor does it mention rigorous statistical significance or temporal robustness tests—both important for predictive claims over years. Related work appears thoroughly covered, and some limitations (stacking data split, noisy older reports) are acknowledged, but key reproducibility details (GARCH implementation moved to supplementary materials; code not mentioned) and analyses of failure modes or formal conditions when embedding-augmentation helps are lacking. Overall the paper is adequate but misses important causal and robustness analyses I expect for strong methodological claims.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper presents a solid empirical evaluation: it compares to several established baselines (Kogan, Wang, Tsai), evaluates multiple fusion strategies (early, stacking, MKL), and includes sectoral analyses and discussion of temporal drift. Ablations/sensitivity are present (fusion method comparison, effect of older years) and hyperparameters are reported, but reproduction is hindered by missing code and the relegation of GARCH implementation details to supplementary materials. Related work is well covered and limitations/negative results are acknowledged, so the study is strong but not perfectly reproducible without additional artifacts.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates measurable, deployment‑ready gains using embedding‑augmented IR and fusion strategies, making it likely to influence practitioners and applied researchers. However, they also note the work is engineering‑focused with limited theoretical analysis and some reproducibility gaps, which constrains its broader, transformative impact; on balance this yields a solid but not groundbreaking contribution, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important applied problem—forecasting equity volatility from 10-K Risk Factors—and demonstrates empirical gains by using word‑embedding–augmented IR sentiment features and by fusing those with market features (improving over GARCH and prior textual baselines). These results are practically relevant and could influence researchers and practitioners working on text‑driven financial prediction and sectoral analyses. However, from a theory‑first perspective the work appears to rely on engineering/heuristic additions (lexicon expansion, IR weighting variants, fusion choices) without formal analysis or complete controlled ablations, which limits its potential to change how the field reasons about when and why embedding‑augmented IR helps; that constrains its impact to a moderate (not transformative) level.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important, practical problem—forecasting volatility from long-form 10-K risk disclosures—and presents engineering-focused advances (embedding-augmented IR term weighting and multiple fusion strategies) that produce measurable gains over established baselines (Kogan, Wang, Tsai) and market-only models. The work is likely to influence practitioners and applied researchers because it demonstrates reproducible, deployment-ready techniques (SVR fusion, sectoral analysis, handling conceptual drift) that improve real-world forecasting accuracy across sectors. It is not purely theoretical but provides concrete methods that others can build on, though lack of mentioned code release and some omitted implementation details limit immediate reproducibility and wider catalytic impact.

main_claims: A word embedding-based information retrieval (IR) sentiment analysis approach for 10-K Risk Factors significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods for volatility prediction (e.g., Kogan et al., Wang et al., Tsai and Wang).

Combining textual sentiment features with market features via different fusion methods (early fusion, stacking/late fusion, and MKL) improves volatility forecasting compared to using market features alone.

Using word embeddings to extend lexicon term weights (by incorporating similar terms) yields better term weighting for long financial documents and reduces noise caused by conceptual drift over years.
The informativeness and characteristics of 10-K reports vary across financial sectors, and this sectoral behavior is investigated in the experiments.
_summary.methodology: Extract Risk Factors from SEC 10-K reports (2006–2015), stem texts and build a Word2Vec model on the corpus to expand a finance-specific lexicon (Loughran & McDonald); apply IR term-weighting schemes extended by embedding-based similar terms to produce textual features, and combine these with market features (current volatility, GARCH, sector one-hot) using early fusion, stacking, and MKL; predictions are made with SVM/SVR (RBF, C=1).
datasets: Yahoo stock prices (used to compute returns/volatility and GARCH)
baselines: Market (market features) using SVM with RBF kernel
limitations: GARCH implementation details omitted and moved to supplementary materials due to lack of space.
Stacking (late fusion) may suffer from lack of training data because the training set is split (70%-30%).
Older reports introduce noise due to conceptual drift; using older years can degrade prediction performance.
_summary.topic_area: NLP / Finance - volatility prediction from financial disclosures

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning and statistical NLP with a PhD in computational linguistics; has led work on formal model properties and theoretical guarantees for text-driven prediction models.
traits: penalises papers that lack formal or theoretical justification for proposed methods (e.g., no analysis of when embedding-augmented IR weighting is expected to help)

requires rigorous statistical significance testing and calibration analyses across time for predictive claims (will deduct points if only mean performance numbers without proper significance or temporal robustness checks are reported)

penalises papers that rely primarily on engineering choices or heuristics without ablation/causal attribution (expects controlled ablations for each claimed contribution, including lexicon expansion, IR weighting variants, and fusion strategies)
favors clarity on model limitations and failure modes (will lower score if no discussion on when/why the method could fail, e.g., conceptual drift or embedding instability over years)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on whether the paper provides principled understanding and controlled attribution of claimed improvements; empirical results must be accompanied by analyses that explain and bound performance.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP & Finance Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Applied researcher with industry and academic experience building NLP systems for financial forecasting and risk; publishes empirical work focused on reproducible benchmarks and domain adaptation for finance.
traits: rewards papers with comprehensive empirical evaluations that include strong baselines, multiple fusion strategies, and sectoral analyses (will give higher score if experiments are extensive and realistic)
values practical impact and domain-specific engineering (will reward methods that improve real-world forecasting accuracy or robustness on long documents like 10-Ks)
prefers reproducibility and public artifacts (will reward papers that release code, data splits, and model parameters or otherwise enable replication)

favors methods that show consistent gains when combined with standard market features and that demonstrate deployment-readiness (will upscore clear improvements over market-only baselines and stability across years/sectors)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I emphasize empirical rigor, realistic baselines, and practical usefulness; I am inclined to accept strong, well-validated applied contributions even if theoretical analysis is light.
